title: Assignment Operators 📝
description: Shortcut operators to update variables quickly.

slides.title: Assignment Shortcuts ⚡
slides.content:
# Compound Assignment Operators

Instead of writing `x = x + 5`, Python lets you use a shortcut: `x += 5`. These are called **compound assignment operators**:

| Operator | Equivalent | Example |
|----------|------------|--------|
| `+=` | `x = x + n` | `x += 5` |
| `-=` | `x = x - n` | `x -= 3` |
| `*=` | `x = x * n` | `x *= 2` |
| `/=` | `x = x / n` | `x /= 4` |
| `//=` | `x = x // n` | `x //= 3` |
| `%=` | `x = x % n` | `x %= 2` |
| `**=` | `x = x ** n` | `x **= 3` |

They do the same thing — just shorter and cleaner! 🧹

slides.title: Shortcuts in Action 🎬
slides.content: Watch how these operators update variables:
slides.codeSnippet:
score = 100
print("Start:", score)

score += 50   # score = 100 + 50
print("+50:", score)

score -= 20   # score = 150 - 20
print("-20:", score)

score *= 2    # score = 130 * 2
print("*2:", score)

score //= 3   # score = 260 // 3
print("//3:", score)

quizData.question: If x = 10, what does x += 5 do?
quizData.explanation: x += 5 is shorthand for x = x + 5. So x becomes 10 + 5 = 15.

slides.title: String Concatenation with += 🔤
slides.content:
## Works with Strings Too!

`+=` isn't just for numbers — it works with strings as well:

```
message = "Hello"
message += " World"
message += "!"
print(message)  # Hello World!
```

This is a common pattern for **building up strings** piece by piece!

quizData.question: What is x after: x = 20; x %= 3?
